coredata.dc:title: Albumin-binding difference caused by hydroxy and bromo on position-2 of benzothiazole
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Yifan
Wang, Honglin
Zhang, Lili
Li, Li
Zhang, Huaxin
coredata.dc:description: Hydroxyl and bromine are important function groups which have been frequently introduced into the molecular structures of active compounds like drugs and pesticides. To investigate the difference of their protein-binding properties, 2-hydroxybenzothiazole (HOB) and 2-bromobenzothiazole (BRB) were selected as ligands to interact with human serum albumin (HSA). The interaction equilibriums were deciphered by multi-spectroscopic detections. Thermodynamic parameters manifested that the recognitions were spontaneous and entropy-driven processes leading by typical hydrophobic forces. The conformational change of HSA was inspected by synchronous emission spectra and circular dichroism techniques. The binding site was confirmed by fluorescence probe strategy. The structure of new protein–ligand complexes was ascertained by molecular docking method, in which the hydrogen bonds as well as molecular orientation of HOB and BRB were involved. The albumin-binding details of HOB and BRB were compared systematically and the difference in energy transfer efficiency and affinity was tentatively explained by density functional theory (DFT) analyses. The study is expected to provide guidance for rational design of novel drugs or pesticides containing benzothiazole scaffold, especially when considering introducing hydroxyl or bromine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2-Hydroxybenzothiazole
2-Bromobenzothiazole
Fluorescence probe
Molecular docking
Thermodynamics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Albumin’s emission spectra quenched by HOB (A) and BRB (B).
Stern-Volmer equations for HOB (A) and BRB systems (B) at three temperatures.
UV absorption of albumin solution with HOB (A) and BRB (B).
logarithmic plotting results of HOB (A) and BRB systems (B).
logK versus 1/T plots of HOB (A) and BRB (B) interacting with albumin.
Influence of HOB (A and B) and BRB (C and D) on synchronous emission.
Impact of HOB and BRB on albumin’ s CD spectrum.
Competitive binding of HOB (A, B) and BRB (C, D) with site markers.
Influence of site markers on binding equilibriums of HOB (A) and BRB (B) with albumin.
The binding location of HOB (A) and BRB (B) in site II.
Microenvironment of HOB (A) and BRB (B) in site II.
Influence of OH and Br substitution on benzothiazole’s albumin-binding property.
Influence of HOB and BRB on secondary structural contents.
Frontier molecular orbitals of HOB and BRB.

head.ce:title: Albumin-binding difference caused by hydroxy and bromo on position-2 of benzothiazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Drug Synthesis and Optimization, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen, Hubei 448000, PR China

Hubei Key Laboratory of Drug Synthesis and Optimization, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen, Hubei 448000, PR China (H. Zhang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydroxyl and bromine are important function groups which have been frequently introduced into the molecular structures of active compounds like drugs and pesticides. To investigate the difference of their protein-binding properties, 2-hydroxybenzothiazole (HOB) and 2-bromobenzothiazole (BRB) were selected as ligands to interact with human serum albumin (HSA). The interaction equilibriums were deciphered by multi-spectroscopic detections. Thermodynamic parameters manifested that the recognitions were spontaneous and entropy-driven processes leading by typical hydrophobic forces. The conformational change of HSA was inspected by synchronous emission spectra and circular dichroism techniques. The binding site was confirmed by fluorescence probe strategy. The structure of new protein–ligand complexes was ascertained by molecular docking method, in which the hydrogen bonds as well as molecular orientation of HOB and BRB were involved. The albumin-binding details of HOB and BRB were compared systematically and the difference in energy transfer efficiency and affinity was tentatively explained by density functional theory (DFT) analyses. The study is expected to provide guidance for rational design of novel drugs or pesticides containing benzothiazole scaffold, especially when considering introducing hydroxyl or bromine.

ce:section.ce:para: Introducing hydroxyl to a drug may enhance its binding affinity to targets, increase its solubility and even change its original bioactivity. Hydroxyl always decreases the activity as well as toxicity if it is located on aliphatic chains, but always increases both of that when placed on aromatic rings because hydroxyl facilitates the binding of ligands to alkaline groups of receptors. Bromine is another familiar member in the structure of numerous industrial products including medicines, pesticides, etc. Aromatic heterocyclic compound 1, 3-benzothiazole is a special molecule because a 3-thiazole ring is fused to a benzene ring, forming a coplanar structure containing two heteroatoms with different types, which makes it exhibit unique biological and pharmaceutical activity superior to common heterocyclic compound [1]. It has been found that 2-substituted benzothiazole derivatives possess higher activity and the property depends a lot on the type of substituents [2]. To investigate the influence of these two important substituents on the bioactivity of benzothiazole, 2-hydroxybenzothiazole (HOB) and 2-bromobenzothiazole (BRB) are established as substrates for comparison.

Since plasma-binding behavior of an exogenous molecule has a significant impact on its pharmacokinetic processes ranging from transportation to distribution [3,4], to absorption and to excretion, one of the fundamental tasks is to probe the protein-binding characteristics when developing a new drug [5]. In present work, human serum albumin (HSA) is designated as a target, which is the most common protein model in evaluating the metabolism action of a drug in vivo [6]. The concrete task of our work is: (1) to determine the recognition of HSA to HOB and BRB respectively, including binding equilibrium, thermodynamic parameter, binding power, high-affinity binding site energy transfer and structure of supramolecular complexes; (2) to compare the albumin-binding affinity of HOB and BRB; and (3) to analyze the difference from the perspective of electronic effect. This study is supposed to be conducive to understanding the structure–activity relationship of benzothiazole compounds when introducing hydroxy and bromo to its position-2, as described in Scheme 1 .

Fluorescence emanating from HSA could be quenched by a variety of substances, which have been utilized to determine the interaction between proteins and substrates [7,8]. Under the same concentration and molar ratios, HOB and BRB exhibited a similar quenching effect on HSA's luminescence, but in different degree. HOB and BRB gave little emission at the fluorescence peak of albumin (Fig. 1 ), which meant the influence of ligands’ emission was negligible. When ligands’ concentration increased to 1.75 × 10−4 mol·L−1, the intensity decreased from 617.2 to 366.1 and 311.6, respectively. Compared to HOB under the same concentration, BRB exhibited stronger quenching ability, which meant the energy transfer efficiency between HSA and BRB was superior to that between HSA and HOB.

Dynamic and static quenching are two typical mechanisms that are commonly used to explain the quenching processes between phosphors and quenchers [9,10]. Dynamic quenching is the result of collision but static one involves complex formation at ground state. After emission intensity data were corrected by Eq. 1 shown in Supporting Information (SI), they were analyzed by Eq. 2 in SI to obtain Stern-Volmer constants [11,12]. Fig. 2 was the fitting results of interaction systems at 288 K, 299 K and 310 K, respectively.

In the Stern-Volmer equation, the slope represents dynamic quenching constant K SV. Fig. 2 showed the value of K SV increase when temperature rises, which seemed to comply with the nature of dynamic quenching. However, it could be detected that the emission peak of albumin solution had moved ca. 8 nm (343 to 335 nm) when ligands’ concentration increased to 1.75 × 10−4 mol·L−1, which implied that there might be some new complex formed. Based on this, ultraviolet spectra were recorded (Fig. 3 ) to verdict the mechanism. It could be noted that, around 220 nm, the superposition curve of HSA and HOB (HSA + HOB) presented higher absorbance than the HSA-HOB system (Fig. 3 A), while HSA + BRB showed a lower peak than the HSA-BRB system (Fig. 3 B), which suggested that new complex had formed between HSA and HOB/BRB. Therefore, the quenching caused by both HOB and BRB should be dominated by static mechanism characterized by complex formation at ground state [13].

To decipher the binding equilibriums, data obtained at varying temperatures were analyzed according to Eq. 3 in SI [14,15]. The double logarithmic plots and the regression equations were recorded in Fig. 4 .

Table 1 showed that the constants between HSA and HOB/BRB were higher than 104, which meant that both HOB-HSA and BRB-HSA complex were stable enough under physiological conditions [18]. Compared with BRB, HOB exhibited stronger binding affinity when binding to HSA at the same temperature. Both of their bindings to HSA were spontaneous (ΔG θ < 0) and entropy-increased. The positive enthalpy changes (ΔH θ) indicated the interactions were endothermic processes, but BRB’s endothermic effect was much more obvious.

Ross and Subramanian [19] found that the binding power between drugs and proteins was closely correlated with thermodynamic results, which had been widely applied to speculate the types of binding power. ΔS θ > 0 usually implies the existence of hydrophobic interaction and electrostatic force; ΔS θ < 0 reflects hydrogen bond and van der Waals force; ΔH θ < 0 always represents electrostatic force. Besides, positive ΔH θ and ΔS θ suggest typical hydrophobic interaction. Based on these rules, the main power dominating the binding of HOB/BRB to albumin was typical hydrophobic interaction.

The peak shift in synchronous fluorescence spectra (SFS) reflects the alteration of polarity or hydrophobicity of chromophore [20]. Fig. 6 presented the influence of HOB/BRB on albumin’s SFS, in which Δλ (λem-λex) was set at 60 nm and 15 nm, corresponding to tryptophan (Trp) and tyrosine (Tyr) residues, respectively [21]. It could be inferred that BRB’ s binding had increased the polarity of the environment around both Trp residue and Tyr residue because of apparent red shifts of their synchronous peaks (Fig. 6 C and D). Nevertheless, with regard to the binding of HOB, the synchronous peaks of 60 nm shifted to short wavelength direction (Fig. 6 A) while peaks of 15 nm moved to long wavelength (Fig. 6 B), suggesting the microenvironment of Trp residue become more hydrophobic while Tyr residue more hydrophilic.

Data in Table 2 showed that free HSA under experimental condition contained approximately 59.8% of α-helix and 5.9% of β-strand. When HOB’s concentration was added to 10 times as high as HSA, α-helix reduced to 57.6% and β-strand increased to 7.1%. Compared with HOB at the same concentration, BRB exhibited more striking changes in α-helix (55.5%) and β-strand (7.9%). The content of unordered structure in HSA-HOB system (21.4%) was also more than HSA-BRB system (22.5%). These indicated BRB had posed greater impact on the secondary structure of HSA, which might also be a sign of greater side effect as a drug or higher activity as a pesticide.

Sudlow [24] had defined two most important binding sites for micro molecule ligands in serum albumin. Warfarin and ibuprofen were the most used binding probes for Sudlow’s site I and Sudlow’s site II [25]. Table 1 showed the value of n was near 1, confirming the existence of one specific binding site. Competitive binding of HOB/BRB and probes was designed to find out where it was (Fig. 8 ).

In our experiments, site markers and HSA were held at equimolar concentration, and then increasing amounts of HOB/BRB was added to HSA-marker. It could be noted from Fig. 8 that warfarin and ibuprofen gave little emission when excited by 285 nm, but only warfarin exhibited a striking quenching effect on HSA's fluorescence. When HOB/BRB was added, the fluorescence of HSA-HOB systems (Fig. 8 A and B) and HSA-BRB systems (Fig. 8 C and D) was gradually quenched, which indicated they could still bind to its specific site whether by directly entering the vacant site or by competing with a marker.

To ascertain the binding site, the quenching data were analyzed by Eq. 3 in SI, using HSA-HOB/HSA-BRB system without site markers as blank. The plots and regression equations were list in Fig. 9 . In Fig. 9, the intercepts of regression equations corresponded to logK b, which indirectly reflected the value of K b. It could be found that when site marker warfarin was in existence, the observed binding constant between HOB and HSA increased, suggesting HOB should not locate at site I (warfarin’s site). However, after adding ibuprofen, the observed K b value decreased, implying HOB was most likely to bind in ibuprofen’s site. Similar results could be observed with regard to BRB, which indicated there was obvious competition between BRB/HOB and ibuprofen in subdomain IIIA of albumin. We speculated that the cyclic structure of benzothiazole was the guider that led BRB/HOB to their binding location. It was interesting that in albumin solutions containing warfarin, the observed K b values between HOB/BRB and HSA had increased. It was probably due to the fact the binding of warfarin on site I had caused conformational alteration which facilitated the entrance of HOB/BRB in site II. On the other hand, it reminded of the potential drug interactions between benzothiazole derivatives and compounds whose structures were similar to warfarin.

He and Cater [26,27] has deciphered the crystallographic structure of HSA, revealing 585 amino acids repeat similar spatial conformation in its molecular structure. Each of its three domains comprises ten helices connected by several stretched peptide chains. Ten helices are divided into two subdomains called subdomain A (the first six helices) and subdomain B (the last four helices), which possess similar structural motifs.

To reveal the concrete interaction modes of ligand in the binding site, molecular modeling was performed [28,29]. Since HOB and BRB had the same binding site, the center of docking grid was set to −12.944, 1.444 and −2.306.

The highest-scored docking results between HOB/BRB and HSA were picked out and shown in Fig. 10 . It showed HOB was located in the left of the pocket formed by subdomain IIIA (Fig. 10 A), while BRB was fixed in the lower right corner (Fig. 10 B).

The optimal conformation of HSA complex with HOB/BRB was written down for further analysis. The residues close to HOB/BRB (within 3.5 Å) were detected and displayed in Fig. 11 . Fig. 11 showed that HOB was surrounded by TYR411, LYS414, VAL415, LEU460, PHE488, SER489 and LEU491 (Fig. 11 A) while BRB was enclosed by a pocket made up of SER342, VAL344, ARG348, PRO384, GLU450, LEU453, SER454 and ARG485. Most of these residues directly interacting with HSA were hydrophobic, proving that the primary power between HOB/BRB and site II was hydrophobic effect. It was in line with the conclusion drawn from thermodynamic analyses.

Meanwhile, the docking results reminded us that no hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) existed between BRB and adjacent residues. However, HOB had formed three H-bonds with site II—two N—H—O between amino in LYS414 and O atom in HOB, one O—H—O between hydroxyl in HOB and carboxylate oxygen in PHE488, which were marked as green dash lines in Fig. 11 A.

On the other hand, TYR 411 was in extremely close proximity to HOB’s hydroxyl terminal, which would increase the polarity of the microenvironment around tyrosine. It was also in line with experimental conclusion drawn from synchronous spectra of tyrosine (Fig. 6 B) in section 3.5.

The main difference in protein-binding properties between HOB and BRB lies in two respects: the binding location and the energy transfer efficiency. The former could be attributed to the existence of hydroxyl which allowed HOB to form H-bonds with amino acid. Nevertheless, the latter might be connected with molecular orbital energy levels of HOB and BRB. Table 3 listed the frontier molecular orbitals of HOB and BRB, as well as their energy levels.

It could be found in Table 3 that E LUMO and E HOMO of BRB were lower than corresponding levels of HOB. The energy difference ΔE (E LUMO-E HOMO) of HOB and BRB were 4.12 and 3.90 eV. The ΔE values of HOB and BRB were both higher than the emission energy of albumin (343 nm corresponds to 3.62 eV). But the ΔE of BRB was relatively close to 3.62 eV, which could explain to some extent why the energy transfer efficiency of HSA-BRB systems was a little higher than HSA-HOB systems. On the other hand, comparing the binding location of HOB (Fig. 10A) and BRB (Fig. 10B) in site II, we could notice that BRB was closer in space to the sole tryptophan residues (TRP214) in HSA, which might be another factor contributing to its higher efficiency as a short distance between donor and receptor would facilitate energy transfer.

The protein-binding characteristics of HOB and BRB were systematically investigated by multiple experimental and theoretical methods including spectroscopic detection, fluorescence probe, molecular modeling and DFT calculation. The difference in binding behaviors was compared and analyzed from different perspectives. The results indicated that both HOB and BRB could form new complex with HSA at ground state through hydrophobic interactions and result in quenching of HSA’s fluorescence. Their interactions with HSA were spontaneous (ΔG θ < 0),entropy-production (ΔS θ > 0) and enthalpy-driven (ΔH θ > 0) processes. Benzothiazole’ s cyclic scaffold made the compounds get into Sudlow’s site II in subdomain IIIA of serum albumin. HOB possessed bigger binding constants (i.e. higher affinity) because its hydroxyl had formed three hydrogen bonds with lysine and phenylalanine in site II. BRB exhibited relatively higher energy transfer efficiency under the same concentration condition as its energy level difference was closer to the energy emitting by chromophore of HSA and its distance to tryptophan residues (TRP214) in space was much closer than HOB. The influence of HOB on polarity of tryptophan and tyrosine was distinctly different from BRB. BRB had posed greater impact on the secondary structure of albumin, implying it might bring greater side effects as a drug or higher activity as a pesticide. The work is expected to facilitate the prediction of the bioactivity of the 2-substituted benzothiazole derivatives and contribute to structural designs of new drugs or pesticides.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research Programs of Jingchu University of Technology (T202101, ZD202105, QN202102, YD202106), Jingmen Science and Technology Projects (ZDCX2017004, 2021ZDYF002), Projects of Hubei Education Department (T2020023, Q20184305) and Porous Materials Preparation & Application Innovation Team Project of Hubei Education Department are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental details and data processing methods to this article are presented in Supporting Information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.116570.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.C. Carter, X.M. He, S.H. Munson, P.D. Twigg, K.M. Gernert, M.B. Broom, T.Y. Miller, Three-dimensional structure of human serum albumin, Science, 244 (1989) 1195-1198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing the interaction of anticancer drug temsirolimus with human serum albumin: molecular docking and spectroscopic insight
J. Biomol. Struct. Dyn.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shamsi, A. Ahmed, B. Bano, Probing the interaction of anticancer drug temsirolimus with human serum albumin: molecular docking and spectroscopic insight, J. Biomol. Struct. Dyn., 36 (2018) 1479-1489.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigating the interaction of anticancer drug temsirolimus with human transferrin: Molecular docking and spectroscopic approach
J. Mol. Recognit.
